claim: EPMA reveals carbon partitioning: austenite in 8Al contains 0.253 wt% C, 6Al austenite contains 0.098 wt% C, while ferrite in 6Al, 8Al, and 10Al shows negligible C; 10Al austenite also shows negligible C.
evidence_text: The compositions of austenite phase and ferrite phase of the designed steels were investigated using the electron probe microanalysis (EPMA). The carbon content in austenite was calculated using the chemical composition lever rule.

claim: The densities of 4Al, 6Al, 8Al, and 10Al steels measured by Archimedes principle are 7.42, 7.18, 6.99, and 6.84 g cm^{-3}, respectively.
evidence_text: The densities of the investigated steels, measured using the principle of Archimedes, are 7.42, 7.18, 6.99, and 6.84 g cm^{-3}, respectively.

claim: The stacking fault energy (γ_SFE) of austenite at room temperature is 51.25 mJ·m^{-2} for 4Al, 62.64 for 6Al, and 68.47 for 8Al; no value is reported for 10Al.
evidence_text: The calculated γ_SFE was recorded in Table 2 by using thermodynamic functions and parameters listed in literature [35].

claim: The ingots were homogenized at 1150 °C for 4 h, hot-forged into φ16.5 mm rods, solution-treated at 1050 °C for 2 h, and oil-quenched to achieve nearly equiaxed grains.
evidence_text: The as-cast ingots were homogenized at 1150 °C for 4 h, and subsequently hot-forged into rods with a diameter of 16.5 mm. The bars were solution-treated at 1050 °C for 2 h, followed by oil quenching, to achieve a microstructure with nearly equiaxed grains.

claim: Tensile tests used standard specimens with 5 mm diameter and 25 mm gauge length at a strain rate of 1.0×10^{-3} s^{-1} at room temperature; interrupted tests were performed at engineering strains of 3%, 10%, 20%, and 28%.
evidence_text: Tensile testing was performed on standard specimens (5 mm in diameter, 25 mm in gauge length) ... with a strain rate of 1.0×10^{-3} s^{-1} at room temperature using an MTS tensile machine. ... Interrupted tensile tests were conducted to engineering strains of 3 %, 10 %, 20 %, and 28 %

claim: EBSD reveals that 4Al is single-phase FCC austenite, 6Al is austenite-based duplex, 8Al and 10Al are ferrite-based duplex; microstructures are quasi-equiaxed with annealing twins in austenite grains.
evidence_text: EBSD analysis indicates that 4Al steel is a single-phase face-centered cubic (FCC) γ austenite lightweight steel, 6Al steel is an austenite-based duplex lightweight steel, and 8Al and 10Al steels are ferrite-based duplex lightweight steels. It also shows that the microstructures with quasi-equiaxed grains are obtained under the solution treatment conditions. It can be observed that the austenite grains contain numerous annealing twin boundaries.

claim: In SADPs along [100]γ, satellite diffraction spots near γ spots indicate spinodal decomposition during quenching; weak spots at {110} positions in 6Al, 8Al, and 10Al are attributed to L1₂-type short-range ordered structure.
evidence_text: The satellite diffraction spots near the γ diffraction spots are related to the concentration fluctuations of Al and C atoms, indicating that spinodal decomposition occurred during quenching. Very weak diffraction spots are identified at the {110} positions in 6Al, 8Al, and 10Al steels. In previous studies, this kind of ordered structure is revealed to be an L1₂ type short-range ordered crystal structure.

claim: In SADPs along [110]α, superlattice reflections with all even Miller indices belong to both B2 and DO3, while those with all odd indices belong only to DO3.
evidence_text: When h, k and l (Miller indices) are all even, the diffraction spots belong to both B2 and DO3. When h, k and l are all odd, the diffraction spots belong only to DO3.

claim: Dark-field TEM images using {002}_DO3 and {111}_DO3 reflections show that DO3 domains dominate the ferritic matrix; regions where {002}_DO3 intensity exceeds {111}_DO3 indicate coexistence of DO3 and B2 domains.
evidence_text: The green arrows show that the dark-field image acquired from the {002}_DO3 reflection exhibits a lower intensity or approximate intensity than that obtained from the {111}_DO3 reflection, indicating the coexistence of main DO3 domains in the ferritic matrix. The yellow arrows indicate that the dark-field image acquired from the {002}_DO3 reflection exhibits a higher intensity than that obtained from the {111} reflection, indicating the coexistence of DO3 domains and B2 domains within the ferritic matrix.

claim: The maximum sizes of DO3 ordered domains are ~2.0 nm in 6Al, ~2.1 nm in 8Al, and ~19 nm in 10Al; domain size increases with Al content, indicating Al promotes DO3 precipitation.
evidence_text: The maximum sizes of DO3 ordered domains in the 6Al, 8Al, and 10Al steels are estimated to be approximately 2.0 nm, 2.1 nm, and 19 nm, respectively. The size of the DO3 ordered domains increases with the increase of the nominal aluminum content, suggesting that Al promotes the precipitation of DO3 ordered domains.

claim: In 10Al steel, acicular 18R martensite precipitates form within ferrite grains during quenching; they are enriched in Mn and C and depleted in Al, and significantly reduce ferrite ductility.
evidence_text: acicular precipitates are observed within the ferrite grains of the 10Al steel, ... The precipitates are identified as 18R martensite formed within the ferrite of the Fe-Mn-Al-C alloy during quenching. This type of acicular martensite can significantly reduce the ductility of ferrite.

claim: Aluminum content strongly controls microstructure: 4Al is nearly full austenite, 6Al is austenite-based duplex, 8Al is ferrite-based duplex, and 10Al is nearly full ferrite; austenite contains L1₂ short-range order and ferrite contains B2/DO3 ordered phases.
evidence_text: Based on above analysis on the microstructure of the low-density steels with different Al content as shown in Fig. 1, it can be concluded that the microstructure of the low-density steel is strongly affected by the aluminum content, giving nearly full austenite microstructure for 4 %Al and 6 %Al, ferrite based duplex microstructure for 8 %Al, but nearly full ferrite microstructure for 10 % AL. Furthermore, in the austenite phase, the short-range ordering of Al and C could be found in the austenite phase of the low-density steel with aluminum evolved from 6 % to 10 %. In the ferrite phase, both B2 and DO3 could be found in the ferrite phase.

claim: The deformation mechanism of the 8Al ferrite-based duplex lightweight steel differs from that of conventional ferrite-based duplex steels.
evidence_text: This indicates that the deformation mechanism of the designed 8Al ferrite-based duplex lightweight steels differs from that of conventional ferrite-based duplex steels [43,44].

claim: 4Al steel has yield strength 239±7 MPa, ultimate tensile strength 552±12 MPa, uniform elongation 38.5±2.0%, and total elongation 52.0±1.8%.
6Al steel has yield strength 251±3 MPa, ultimate tensile strength 589±18 MPa, uniform elongation 37.6±0.9%, and total elongation 51.6±1.5%.
8Al steel has yield strength 455±10 MPa, ultimate tensile strength 740±14 MPa, uniform elongation 31.0±1.3%, and total elongation 39.8±1.8%.
10Al steel has yield strength 585±29 MPa, ultimate tensile strength 613±41 MPa, uniform elongation 2.1±1.1%, and total elongation 4.6±1.6%.
At 3% engineering strain, planar dislocation slip along slip planes and distinct cross-slip are observed in the austenite.
evidence_text: When the engineering strain reaches 3 %, planar dislocation slip along the slip planes and distinct cross-slip are observed in the austenite (Fig. 5 (a))

claim: Using two-beam diffraction near [111] zone axis and g·b analysis, the Burgers vector of dislocations in ferrite of 10Al steel pre-deformed to 3% strain is determined as a/2[111]; dislocations lose contrast under g = [10 1] and g = [1 -1 0] and are all visible under g = [0 -1 1].
evidence_text: When g = [10 1] and g = [1 -1 0] operating vector, some long straight dislocations systematically lose contrast (Fig. 7(a and b)), whereas when g = [0 -1 1] operating vector, all dislocations are visible (Fig. 7(c)). It can be concluded that the Burgers vector b is determined to be a/2[111] by the g·b = 0 analysis of the ferrite in 10Al steel, as shown in Fig. 7(a–c).

claim: The approximately 24.3% austenite in 8Al steel, acting as a soft phase, effectively alleviates stress localization within the ferrite and enhances the deformation of the ferrite to higher stresses; edge dislocations are more readily activated at the ferrite-austenite grain boundaries due to high stress.
evidence_text: It can be observed that edge dislocations are more readily activated at the grain boundaries, which can be explained by the high stress present at these boundaries. ... Additionally, approximately 24.3 % austenite in 8Al steel, acting as a soft phase, can effectively alleviate stress localization within the ferrite and enhance the deformation of the ferrite to higher stresses.

claim: The large-sized D03 ordered domains in 10Al steel cannot be completely sheared, whereas the D03 ordered domains in 8Al steel can be completely sheared along the dislocation line direction and the slip plane direction.
evidence_text: the large sized DO3 ordered domains in 10Al steel cannot be completely sheared (Fig. 8(c)), while the DO3 ordered domains in 8Al steel can be completely sheared along the dislocation line direction and the slip plane direction (Fig. 8(f)).

claim: When D03 ordered domains are destroyed by dislocations, anti-phase boundaries (APBs) are left behind, and the attractive force generated by APB energy leads to dislocation pairing; APB energy increases with Al content in Fe-Al alloys, causing a substantially stronger attractive force and more pronounced dislocation pairing in 10Al ferrite compared to 8Al ferrite.
evidence_text: When the DO3 ordered domains are destroyed by dislocations, anti-phase boundaries (APBs) are left behind [16]. The attractive force generated by APB energy leads to the pairing of dislocations. Studies on Fe-Al alloys demonstrate that APB energy increases with Al content [14,55].

claim: The calculated D03 ordered domain size in ferrite of 10Al steel (3.0 nm) is significantly smaller than the TEM-observed value of 19 nm, whereas the calculated size for 8Al steel (1.5 nm) is very close to its TEM-observed value of 2.1 nm, indicating that ordered domains in 8Al steel are more readily sheared.
evidence_text: the calculated DO3 ordered domain size in ferrite of 10Al steel is significantly smaller than the TEMobserved value of 19 nm, whereas the calculated size for 8Al steel is very close to its TEM-observed value of 2.1 nm.

claim: Inverse fast Fourier transforms (IFFT) performed on high-resolution images at positions 1 and 2 in Fig. 8(g) along (011) and (01-1) planes reveal that dislocations are composed of a multitude of dislocation dipoles, and the D03 superlattice is sheared along dislocation line and slip plane by these dislocation dipoles.
evidence_text: inverse fast Fourier transforms (IFFT) are performed on the high-resolution images at positions 1 and 2 in Fig. 8(g) along the (01 1) and (0 1 1) planes, as shown in Fig. 8(h and i). It can be observed that dislocations are composed of a multitude of dislocation dipoles, and the DO3 superlattice is sheared along dislocation line and slip plane by a large number of these dislocation dipoles.

claim: Inverse fast Fourier transform (IFFT) analysis of high-resolution images in Fig. 8(g) along (01̅1) and (011) planes reveals that dislocations are composed of a multitude of dislocation dipoles, and the DO3 superlattice is sheared along the dislocation line and slip plane by these dipoles.
evidence_text: It can be observed that dislocations are composed of a multitude of dislocation dipoles, and the D O 3 superlattice is sheared along dislocation line and slip plane by a large number of these dislocation dipoles.

claim: The observation of sheared DO3 superlattice by dislocation dipoles is in agreement with previous reports where sheared LRO clusters are proposed to cause planar slip.
evidence_text: These findings are in agreement with observations reported in Refs. [12,13,30,31] where sheared LRO clusters are proposed to cause planar slip.

claim: In stage B, a reduction in the strain hardening rate is caused by the slip plane softening effect induced by dislocation shearing of the DO3 long-range ordered domains.
evidence_text: In stage B, a reduction in the strain hardening rate is caused by the slip plane softening effect induced by dislocation shearing of the D O 3 long-range ordered domains [13].

claim: In stage C, the passing stress generated by slip bands of stored dislocations is the primary contributor to strain hardening; with increasing strain the number of slip bands increases, the spacing between slip bands decreases, and the passing stress rises, causing an increase in the strain hardening rate.
evidence_text: In stage C, the passing stress generated by slip bands of stored dislocations has been demonstrated to be the primary contributor to strain hardening [12]. With the increase of strain, the number of slip bands increases, the spacing between slip bands decreases, and the passing stress rises, which leads to an increase in the strain hardening rate.

claim: In stage E, localization of stress results in a rapid decline in the strain hardening rate and leads to plastic failure.
evidence_text: In stage E, localization of stress results in a rapid decline in the strain hardening rate and leads to plastic failure.

claim: The total flow stress σ_tot is expressed as σ_tot = σ_0 + σ(ε), where σ_0 is the deformation-independent strengthening contribution (yield stress) and σ(ε) characterizes the evolution of flow stress during plastic deformation.
evidence_text: In dislocation-mediated plastic materials, the total flow stress σ_t o t can be expressed by the following equation [57,58]: σ_t o t = σ_0 + σ(ε) where σ_0 represents the deformation-independent strengthening contribution, i.e., the yield stress, and the strain hardening term σ(ε) characterizes the evolution of flow stress during plastic deformation.

claim: The strain hardening term σ(ε) is partitioned by phase mass fractions: σ(ε) = 0.757 σ_α(ε) + 0.243 σ_γ(ε), where 75.7% and 24.3% are the mass fractions of ferrite and austenite, respectively.
evidence_text: The strain hardening term σ(ε) is composed of two parts, ferrite and austenite, and can be expressed as follows: σ(ε) = 0 . 7 5 7 σ_α(ε) + 0 . 2 4 3 σ_γ(ε) where 75.7 % and 24.3 % represent the mass fractions of ferrite (α) and austenite (γ), respectively.

claim: The ferrite strain hardening contribution from slip band refinement is determined by σ_α(ε) = K M G b / D, where K is a geometrical factor, M = 2.8 is the Taylor-factor, G = 80.7 GPa is the shear modulus, b = 0.25 nm is the Burgers vector magnitude, and D is the mean slip band spacing.
evidence_text: The ferrite strain hardening contribution from slip band refinement is determined through passing stress analysis [12,59]: σ_(α) (ε) = K M G b / D where K is a geometrical factor, M = 2.8 [56,60] is the Taylor-factor, G = 80.7 GPa [56] is the shear modulus, b = 0.25 nm [56] is the magnitude of the Burgers vector, and D is the mean slip band spacing (Fig. 6).

claim: The austenite strain hardening contribution arises from slip band, dislocation cell block, and dislocation cell refinement and is given by σ_γ(ε) = K_SB M_SB G b / D + 0.8 K_DCB M_DCB G b / D + 0.2 K_DC M_DC G b / D, with M_SB = M_DCB = 3.06, M = 2.44, G = 70 GPa, b = 0.25 nm, and area fractions 0.8 for DCB and 0.2 for DC.
evidence_text: The austenite strain hardening contribution arising from slip band, dislocation cell block, and dislocation cell refinement is derived from an empirical relationship [45,46,61]: σ_(γ) (ε) = K_S B M_S B G b / D + 0 . 8 K_D C B M_D C B G b / D + 0 . 2 K_D C M_D C G b / D ... M_S B = M_D C B = 3.06, M = 2.44 ... G = 70 GPa ... b = 0.25 nm ... The values 0.8 and 0.2 are the area fractions of the dislocation cell blocks and the dislocation cells.

claim: From the relation for 4Al steel in Fig. 9(a), the similitude constants are determined as K_SB+DCB = 2.03 and K_DC = 2.02.
evidence_text: Fig. 9 (a) presents the relation (8) for the 4Al steel, from which the similitude constant K is obtained as K_SB + D C B = 2 . 0 3 , K_D C = 2 . 0 2 .

claim: The best fit between calculated and measured flow stress for 8Al steel is achieved with K = 0.75 and σ_0 = 455 MPa.
evidence_text: It can be seen that the best fit between the calculations and experiments is achieved when K = 0 . 7 5 and σ_0 = 4 5 5 M P a .

claim: The fitted K value of 0.75 for 8Al steel is very close to the similitude coefficient K = 0.7 for solid solution Fe30.4Mn8Al1.2C (wt%) austenite lightweight steel, indicating that the ferrite slip bands have undergone the dynamic slip band refinement (DSBR) effect.
evidence_text: This K = 0 . 7 5 value is very close to the similitude coefficient K = 0 . 7 for solid solution Fe30.4Mn8Al1.2C (wt%) austenite lightweight steel [12]. This further indicates that the ferrite slip bands have undergone the dynamic slip band refinement (DSBR) effect [11,12].

claim: At engineering strains of 3.0%, 10.0%, 20.0%, and 28.0% (true strains 3.0%, 9.5%, 18.2%, 24.7%), the ferrite strain hardening contribution σ_α(ε) is 41, 104, 260, 347 MPa and the austenite contribution σ_γ(ε) is 28, 67, 79, 111 MPa.
evidence_text: Table 4 The strengthening contribution of σ_(α) (ε) and σ_(γ) (ε) strengthening to the flow stress. Engineering strain (%) 3.0 10.0 20.0 28.0 True strain, % 3.0 9.5 18.2 24.7 True stress, MPa 528 662 829 924 σ_(α)(ε), MPa 41 104 260 347 σ_(γ)(ε), MPa 28 67 79 111

claim: This study reveals a new ductility-enhancing mechanism of ferrite+ austenite duplex steel and provides a new route for designing BCC-based low-density steel with high strength and high ductility.
evidence_text: This study not only reveals a new ductility-enhancing mechanism of ferrite + austenite duplex steel, but also provides a new route for designing BCC based low-density steel with high strength and high ductility.
